# Centrality Interpretation

Centrality identifies important nodes in a network.

## Degree centrality

`degree(i) = number of connected neighbours`

In coordination graphs, high degree can mean an account repeatedly co-acts with many others.

## Weighted degree / strength

`strength(i) = Σ_j w_ij`

This is often more useful than degree because repeated coordination matters.

## Betweenness centrality

Measures how often a node lies on shortest paths between other nodes.

High betweenness can indicate:

- broker account
- cross-cluster amplifier
- agency-like coordinator
- bridge between campaign cells

## Eigenvector centrality

A node scores high if it connects to other high-scoring nodes.

Useful for identifying central actors in influential or dense clusters.

## Caution

A high-centrality node can be benign:

- a legitimate official account
- a news source
- a popular creator
- a public launch page

Always examine actor identity, disclosure, and content.
